title: Find two-sum indices
planQuestions: What makes two indices different even when their values match?
What information from earlier values could make a later match fast?
Which input proves a single number cannot pair with itself?

hints.text: The two items must occupy different positions in the array.
hints.concept: A matching value is not enough if it comes from the same item.

hints.text: What could you remember about values already seen while moving left to right?
hints.concept: Earlier values can be recorded with their positions.
hints.lookAt: The partner lookup inside the array loop.

hints.text: Inspect how the current lookup behaves when the target is twice the current value.
hints.concept: A lookup can accidentally return the current index.
hints.lookAt: The function that finds a partner index.
hints.testIdea: Try [3, 4] with target 6, then [3, 3] with target 6.

explainBackQuestion: Why must a two-sum lookup distinguish a matching value from a second array item?

brief.story: A budget helper looks for two different entries that add to a target. One entry cannot be used twice just because its value would complete the sum.
brief.desiredBehavior: Return the indices of two distinct values that add to the target, or null when no such pair exists.
acceptanceCriteria: Returned indices refer to two different array positions.
A repeated value may form a pair only when it appears twice.
Return null when no valid pair exists.
constraints: Work in the TypeScript Kata Lab library.
Keep the first valid pair in left-to-right discovery order.
brief.example: [3, 3] with target 6 returns [0, 1]; [3, 4] with target 6 returns null.
